title: Recognising and remediating a failing cold-deck flat roof
subtitle: Condensation, rot, and insulation failure in cold-deck construction, and what to do about it
excerpt: Most UK flat roofs built before 2000 are cold-deck construction: insulation sits between the joists below the deck boards, leaving a ventilated space above. When ventilation fails, condensation forms on the underside of the deck, the insulation saturates, and rot follows. This explains how to diagnose the problem and what the remediation options are.
sourceNotes: gov.uk Building Regulations Approved Document L and Approved Document C (moisture) for flat roof construction guidance. BRE Good Building Guide 25 on flat roof insulation. NHBC Standards flat roof chapter. Energy Saving Trust guidance on flat roof refurbishment.

glossaryTerms.definition: A membrane, typically polyethylene sheet or foil-backed board, installed on the warm side of insulation to retard the passage of water vapour and reduce interstitial condensation risk.
Thermal transmittance: the rate of heat flow through a building element per unit area per degree of temperature difference, in W/m²·K. Lower is better.
Polyisocyanurate rigid insulation board. Higher performance per millimetre than mineral wool: lambda ~0.022 W/m·K, so 25 mm of PIR delivers roughly the same resistance as 55 mm of mineral wool batts.

content.text: A cold-deck flat roof has insulation between the joists with a ventilated gap above. Cold outside air is meant to circulate through the void and carry away moisture. In practice, the ventilation gaps clog with debris or were never sized correctly. Within a few years the deck boards are wet underneath, the insulation is saturated, and the joists begin to decay.

A saturated cold-deck flat roof is simultaneously a structural problem, a heat-loss problem, and a damp problem. The roof covering may look intact from outside while the structure beneath is failing. Thermal imaging on a cold evening shows dark patches on the ceiling below where the saturated insulation transmits heat faster than the dry sections.
Diagnosing the failure
Damp patches on the ceiling below the flat roof after cold weather but no rain: internal condensation, not a leak.
Damp patches after rain: roof covering has failed, or a flashing has lifted. This is a leak, not a condensation problem, and must be fixed before any insulation work.
Cold ceiling and poor thermal comfort in the room below: insulation has failed or settled.
Probe the deck boards where you can reach them (through a hatch or by lifting a felt corner). A probe or awl sinking more than 5 mm without resistance means soft, decayed wood.
The remediation options

Option 1: re-ventilate and replace insulation. If the deck boards are structurally sound, it is possible to clear the ventilation paths, replace the saturated insulation with new mineral wool batts, and add a
vapour control layer
on the warm side of the insulation. This extends the roof life but does not convert it to warm-deck construction. It remains vulnerable if the ventilation path later blocks again.
Option 2: over-deck warm-roof conversion. Strip the failed covering, add a layer of
PIR board

on top of the existing deck, and apply a new waterproof covering over the PIR. The insulation moves to the warm side of the deck; there is no cold zone where condensation can form. This is the more permanent solution and achieves a better
U-value

. For a typical 20 m² kitchen flat roof, 120 mm of PIR achieves U = 0.18 W/m²·K. Over-decking adds 120-150 mm of build-up to the roof perimeter, which may require flashing adjustments at parapet walls and door thresholds.

Option 3: demolish and rebuild. If the deck boards are structurally compromised and the joists show decay, the roof must be rebuilt. This is an opportunity to specify warm-deck construction from the outset.
Worked example: 1970s detached, 20 m² kitchen flat roof
Existing construction: 100 mm joists, 50 mm of glass wool (now saturated), 25 mm ventilated void, 18 mm chipboard deck, failed felt roofing.
Current U-value (saturated insulation): approximately 0.7 W/m²·K. Warm day heat loss: negligible. Cold day heat loss: significant because the insulation transmits heat rather than resisting it.
Over-deck warm-roof conversion with 120 mm PIR and single-ply membrane: U = 0.18 W/m²·K. Annual saving vs failed cold deck: approximately 900 kWh, about 55 pounds at 6p/kWh gas.
When to call a professional

Always use a specialist flat-roofing contractor for the waterproof membrane. Poor detailing at abutments and flashings causes water ingress within a year. Get three quotes from contractors who have done single-ply membrane work before. Check that they are BBA or KIWA-approved for the membrane product they plan to use.
Done well vs done badly

Done well: the new warm-deck construction has continuous insulation from the parapet to the abutment wall, no gaps at upstands, correct fall toward the outlet (minimum 1:80), and a membrane guaranteed for at least 20 years. The ceiling below is dry and warm on cold nights.
Done badly: insulation boards with gaps at joints, parapet flashings left at the old height so water ponds behind the upstand, and cheap felt that fails within five years.

attrs.symptom: New flat roof leaks at a door threshold
attrs.cause: The door sill was at the original roof surface level; adding PIR board has raised the surface above the sill
attrs.fix: A step-down threshold detail or a drainage channel at the door must be built into the design before any board goes down. Retrospective remediation is expensive.

attrs.symptom: Ceiling below still shows cold patches after warm-deck conversion
attrs.cause: Ventilation grilles from the old cold-deck void are still open, allowing cold air into the joist cavity below the deck
attrs.fix: Seal the old eaves ventilation grilles permanently. The warm-deck does not require ventilation; the grilles are vestigial.
